_meta.note: CARBONsnaps data. Instrument prices and stories updated weekly. Regulatory tracker updated continuously.

tier_guide.daily: Update every trading day — spot prices, spreads
tier_guide.weekly: Update weekly — price consensus, regulatory signals, stories
tier_guide.structural: Update on major regulatory decision or market structure change

instruments.name: EU Allowances
instruments.description: European Union Emissions Trading System allowances. Each permit covers one tonne of CO2. The world's largest carbon market.
instruments.regulatory_note: Market Stability Reserve review ongoing. Outcome expected Q3 2026.

story.beginner: EU carbon allowances (EUAs) have fallen sharply, dropping nearly 9% in just one week and losing almost a quarter of their value over three months. This means it is becoming cheaper for companies to buy permission to emit carbon dioxide in Europe, which typically happens when markets worry about lower industrial activity or oversupply.
story.moderate: EUA prices have contracted significantly across all timeframes, with the steepest decline (23.7%) occurring over three months, signalling bearish sentiment on near-term compliance demand. The weakness reflects softer economic outlook expectations and potential supply concerns ahead of the Market Stability Reserve (MSR) review due in Q3 2026, which could alter the mechanics of how allowances are released into the market. Current pricing at 62.67 EUR/tonne sits at multi-month lows, testing support levels that may trigger tactical positioning shifts.
story.expert: EUA weakness has accelerated, with the -8.9% weekly decline compressing into a -23.7% three-month drawdown, consistent with deteriorating macroeconomic signals and reduced EU industrial output forecasts. The MSR review scheduled for Q3 2026 introduces regulatory uncertainty; market participants are pricing in the possibility of increased supply or reduced scarcity mechanics, which could structurally underpin prices near current levels. The cloudy regulatory signal reflects ambiguity around whether the review will tighten or relax the reserve's intake/release thresholds, making forward positioning contingent on Q2 2026 policy signals. Compliance demand remains structurally supportive, but the three-month price action indicates hedging pressure and speculative de-risking dominating near-term microstructure.

instruments.name: California Carbon Allowances
instruments.description: California Cap-and-Trade allowances under the CARB program. Covers ~85% of California GHG emissions.
instruments.regulatory_note: 2026 cap adjustment under CARB review. Floor price mechanism provides downside support.

story.beginner: California Carbon Allowances (CCAs) are permits that allow companies to emit one tonne of greenhouse gas. They're currently trading at $28.32 per tonne, down 1.5% over the past week and month. The price has stayed relatively stable because California's rules include a price floor that prevents them from falling too far.
story.moderate: CCAs have declined 1.5% over one week and one month, holding above the regulatory floor price of $20 per tonne, which provides a mechanical support level in the California cap-and-trade system. The modest downward pressure reflects uncertainty around the 2026 cap adjustment currently under California Air Resources Board (CARB) review, which will reset the total allowances available for the next compliance period. With regulatory signals cloudy, the market is likely pricing in cautious sentiment ahead of clarity on stringency and supply allocation.
story.expert: CCAs have printed consistent 1.5% losses across all measured timeframes, settling at $28.32/tonne with the regulatory floor (currently $20) providing technical support. The uncertainty driver is CARB's ongoing review of the 2026 cap adjustment, which will determine post-2025 scarcity and marginal abatement costs for the compliance market. The flat decline pattern and cloudy signal suggest the market is neither repricing supply tightness nor signalling demand contraction, likely indicating positioning consolidation ahead of cap announcement. Watch for CARB guidance on cap stringency and allowance allocation rules as a near-term catalyst; a deeper cut could reignite upward pressure, whilst a shallow adjustment may test the floor.

instruments.name: UK Allowances
instruments.description: UK Emissions Trading Scheme allowances. Launched in 2021 post-Brexit as a standalone system.
instruments.regulatory_note: UK-EU ETS linking feasibility study due 2026. Outcome would significantly affect pricing.

story.beginner: UK Allowances (UKA) are tradeable permits that allow companies to emit carbon dioxide in the UK. The price has fallen to 34.55 GBP per tonne this week, down 2.7% from last week and 15.5% over three months, suggesting weakening demand or oversupply in the market.
story.moderate: UK Allowances have declined 5.9% over the month and 15.5% over three months, reaching 34.55 GBP/tonne, reflecting softer sentiment in the compliance segment. The downward pressure may reflect expectations around the EU-UK ETS linking feasibility study due in 2026, which could restructure the market significantly. Until clarity emerges on linkage terms, price discovery remains uncertain and participants are likely adopting cautious positioning.
story.expert: UKA has retested lower levels at 34.55 GBP/tonne following a 15.5% three-month decline, with near-term momentum decidedly bearish. The regulatory backdrop remains cloudy ahead of the 2026 EU-UK linking feasibility study, a material event that could fundamentally alter supply-demand dynamics and allowance value through potential mutual recognition, border carbon adjustment interaction, or recalibrated scarcity assumptions. The current price action suggests the market is pricing in incremental dilution risk from linking, whilst deferred long positioning signals reduced conviction among compliance hedgers. Forward curves likely remain inverted or flattened pending study outcomes.

instruments.name: CORSIA Credits
instruments.description: Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation. ICAO-governed global mechanism requiring airlines to offset emissions growth above 2019 baseline.
instruments.regulatory_note: Phase 1 demand growing as aviation recovers. Eligible fuels criteria update expected mid-2026.

story.beginner: CORSIA credits cost around 6.40 dollars per tonne. Airlines must buy these credits to offset the growth in their CO2 emissions above 2019 levels. As air travel keeps recovering from COVID lows, airlines need more credits, pushing prices higher.
story.moderate: CORSIA credits at 6.40 USD/tonne have risen 12% over three months as aviation demand recovery drives offsetting obligations higher. Phase 1 (2024-2026) covers voluntary participants, with Phase 2 (2027+) becoming mandatory. The eligible fuels criteria update mid-2026 is worth watching: if ICAO broadens the list of qualifying credits, supply could increase and cap price appreciation. Supply is currently constrained by the narrow list of CORSIA-eligible credit types.
story.expert: CORSIA Phase 1 demand is tracking above initial ICAO projections as RPK recovery exceeds the 2019 baseline faster than modelled. The credit price at 6.40 USD/tonne reflects a supply-constrained market: only CORSIA-eligible credits (predominantly Gold Standard and Verra credits meeting specific additionality criteria) qualify, limiting the supply pool to approximately 150-200Mt annually against growing demand. Phase 2 mandatory participation from 2027 introduces a structural demand step-change. The eligible fuels update is a binary event: expansion of qualifying methodologies could add 50-80Mt of supply, capping the price; a restrictive outcome tightens supply further.

instruments.name: California LCFS Credits
instruments.description: Low Carbon Fuel Standard credits generated by producing or blending fuels below California's carbon intensity benchmark.
instruments.regulatory_note: Prices under pressure. CARB 2030 pathway consultation could reset the carbon intensity benchmark and restore credit value.

story.beginner: California LCFS credits, which reward cleaner fuels, have fallen to $57 per credit as the market faces uncertainty. The state is consulting on new rules that could change how credits are valued, so traders are holding back until they know what's coming.
story.moderate: LCFS credits have declined 1.7% over one week, one month, and three months, suggesting sustained selling pressure as market participants await CARB's 2030 pathway consultation. The regulatory signal is stormy: any material reset to the carbon intensity benchmark could alter credit generation rates and scarcity dynamics. Current weakness reflects hesitation ahead of potential rule changes that may either restore or further compress credit values.
story.expert: LCFS credits at $57/credit display flat momentum across all measured timeframes (one week, one month, three months all at -1.7%), indicating a sustained liquidation pattern rather than panic selling. The regulatory backdrop is acute: CARB's 2030 pathway consultation poses material execution risk to the current compliance framework, and the market is correctly pricing in information asymmetry around whether the consultation will tighten the carbon intensity benchmark (supportive for credit scarcity) or loosen it (bearish for valuations). Positioning remains cautious; the stormy regulatory signal suggests participants are avoiding fresh long accumulation until the consultation outcomes are clarified, leaving the instrument vulnerable to further pressure if headline policy signals disappoint the bullish consensus.

instruments.name: Renewable Fuel Standard Credits
instruments.description: Renewable Identification Numbers under the EPA Renewable Fuel Standard. D3 (cellulosic) and D6 (conventional ethanol) are the benchmark categories.
instruments.regulatory_note: EPA 2026 volume obligations final rule pending. Obligated parties watching for RVO levels.

story.beginner: RINs are credits that oil refiners must buy to prove they have blended enough renewable fuel into petrol and diesel. The D6 benchmark currently trades at around 68 cents. When the EPA sets higher blending requirements, refiners need more RINs and prices rise.
story.moderate: D6 RINs at 0.68 USD are range-bound ahead of the EPA's 2026 Renewable Volume Obligation final rule. The RVO level is the primary price driver: a higher obligation requires refiners to surrender more RINs, tightening supply and pushing prices up. D3 (cellulosic) RINs command a significant premium at approximately 2.80 USD, reflecting scarcer supply from advanced biofuel producers. Small refinery exemptions remain a wildcard: if the EPA grants more exemptions, effective demand for RINs falls.
story.expert: D6 RINs at 0.68 USD are trading at the lower end of the post-2022 range, reflecting ample ethanol production and carry-forward RIN bank balances. The 2026 RVO is the binary catalyst: EPA proposals released in Q4 2025 suggested a modest obligation increase, which the market has partially priced. Small refinery exemption volumes (totalling approximately 2.5bn gallons in recent years) remain a meaningful uncertainty. D3 RINs at 2.80 USD offer a more interesting risk/reward given the structural supply constraint on cellulosic fuel production and the political tailwinds for advanced biofuel mandates under current energy policy.

instruments.name: 45Z Clean Fuel Credits
instruments.description: IRA-created production tax credit for clean transportation fuels. Replaces the Blenders Tax Credit from 2025. Value depends on the carbon intensity of the fuel produced.
instruments.regulatory_note: IRS guidance finalisation pending. High uncertainty around credit calculation methodology and eligible fuel types.

story.beginner: The 45Z credit is a new US government incentive that pays fuel producers for making cleaner transportation fuels. The cleaner the fuel, the larger the credit. It is still new and the exact rules are not fully finalised, creating uncertainty for fuel producers planning investments.
story.moderate: 45Z replaced the Blenders Tax Credit in 2025 and is structured as a production credit rather than a blending credit, shifting the incentive from blenders to producers. The credit value is inversely linked to carbon intensity: fuels below 50g CO2e per MJ qualify, with higher credits for lower-CI fuels. IRS guidance finalisation is the critical near-term event. Uncertainty around the GREET model update and international feedstock eligibility has delayed investment decisions across the sustainable aviation fuel and renewable diesel sectors.
story.expert: 45Z represents the most significant structural change to US clean fuel incentives since the RFS. As a CI-based production credit, it creates a direct link between carbon intensity scoring and credit value, incentivising feedstock optimisation rather than volume blending. The IRS guidance gap is creating material investment uncertainty: SAF producers using international feedstocks (HEFA-SPK from imported tallow, for example) have unresolved eligibility questions that affect project economics by 40-60 cents per gallon. Resolution of the GREET pathway question for international feedstocks is the single most important near-term clarification for the SAF investment market.

instruments.name: Voluntary Carbon Credits
instruments.description: Nature-based voluntary carbon credits verified under Verra VCS or Gold Standard. REDD+, reforestation, and soil carbon project types. Prices vary by vintage, project type, and co-benefits.
instruments.regulatory_note: Verra VM0042 REDD+ methodology update active. Article 6 bilateral agreements could redirect supply toward compliance markets.

story.beginner: Voluntary carbon credits are environmental certificates that companies buy to offset their emissions outside of government regulations. The price of nature-based credits has risen 2.5% over the past week, month, and three months, suggesting modest buyer demand as climate commitments remain in focus.
story.moderate: Nature-based voluntary carbon credits (VCM) have appreciated 2.5% across all measured timeframes, settling at USD 6.5/tonne. The steady gains reflect stable demand from corporate net-zero commitments, though the regulatory environment remains uncertain. Verra's updated REDD+ methodology and potential Article 6 bilateral agreements could shift supply allocation toward compliance markets, which may tighten voluntary supply pools if governments prioritise international emissions trading.
story.expert: VCM nature-based credits are consolidating gains of 2.5% (week/month/three-month parity), indicating consistent buyer interest at the USD 6.5/tonne level despite cloudy regulatory signals. The Verra VM0042 REDD+ methodology refresh and emerging Article 6 bilateral frameworks create structural uncertainty: if Article 6 gains traction among jurisdictions, supply reallocation toward compliance markets could compress voluntary availability, particularly for high-integrity forest carbon. Current pricing may underestimate tail risk should Article 6 accelerate or methodology tightening materially constrain eligible project issuance. Monitor compliance market absorption rates and bilateral negotiation timelines as leading indicators of VCM supply pressure.

events.title: EU ETS Phase 4 Linear Reduction Factor increase
events.summary: The EU ETS Linear Reduction Factor increased from 2.2% to 4.3% from 2024 onwards (rising to 4.4% in 2028). This accelerates the annual decline in the cap and is the primary long-run structural support for EUA prices.
events.analyst_note: The LRF increase from 2.2% to 4.3% is already law and in effect. The structural case for EUA is intact: the cap tightens faster, fewer allowances exist each year, and covered sectors have limited short-run abatement options. This is the floor beneath any bullish EUA thesis.

scenarios.rationale: The LRF increase to 4.3% is established law and provides structural support through accelerated cap tightening, though industrial demand remains uncertain given economic conditions.
Strong industrial recovery combined with the structural cap tightening could drive significant upward pressure as covered sectors face limited short-term abatement options.
Prolonged economic weakness reducing industrial emissions demand could temporarily offset the structural tightening effects of the higher LRF.

events.title: CARB LCFS re-adoption and 2030 CI benchmark tightening
events.summary: CARB re-adopted the LCFS in 2024 with tighter carbon intensity benchmarks through 2030 designed to restore credit value following structural oversupply from EV charging credits. New pathways and reporting requirements also introduced. Bullish for LCFS credit prices.
events.analyst_note: This is already in effect. The 2024 amendments took full effect July 2025 with a 9% step-change in CI benchmark, turning the credit bank from net surplus to net deficit in Q3 2025. CARB projects credit prices recovering toward $100 to $200 per credit. The structural oversupply that depressed prices since 2022 is unwinding. The re-adoption is the single most important near-term driver of LCFS price recovery.
events.source_label: CARB, Low Carbon Fuel Standard Regulation Amendment, Board approved 8 November 2024; OAL effective July 2025

scenarios.rationale: Tightened CI benchmarks should gradually reduce credit bank surplus whilst market participants adapt to the modified regulatory framework over the coming quarters.
Credit bank depletion accelerates as the 9% CI step-change generates significant deficit obligations, potentially triggering the automatic adjustment mechanism earlier than expected.
Credit oversupply persists longer than anticipated due to continued renewable diesel imports and EV charging credit generation despite the amended CI targets.

events.title: IRS 45Z clean fuel production credit guidance
events.summary: The 45Z credit became effective January 2025 replacing the biodiesel tax credit. IRS issued interim guidance on lifecycle methodology (GREET-based). Final guidance on feedstock-specific pathways and sustainable aviation fuel definitions is pending and will determine credit values for key fuel types.
events.analyst_note: The 45Z credit is live but the lifecycle methodology guidance for key fuel pathways, including sustainable aviation fuel and hydrogen, remains interim. Final IRS guidance will determine which feedstocks qualify at which credit values. Uncertainty is currently depressing investment in some pathways. Resolution is bullish for producers with qualifying fuels. Confidence is High on the credit existing; Medium on the specific pathway values.
events.source_label: IRS/Treasury, Notice 2025-10 and Notice 2025-11, Section 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit Guidance, 10 January 2025

scenarios.rationale: The 45Z guidance appears likely to proceed with interim methodologies maintaining current investment uncertainty whilst establishing a functioning regulatory framework.
Final guidance could clarify favourable pathways for sustainable aviation fuel and advanced biofuels, accelerating investment decisions and credit utilisation.
Potential regulatory delays under new administration could extend uncertainty and postpone investment commitments in qualifying fuel pathways.

events.title: CBAM definitive phase launch
events.summary: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism moves from transitional reporting to full financial adjustment in January 2026. Importers of cement, steel, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, and hydrogen must purchase CBAM certificates. Free allocation for covered sectors begins phasing out in parallel.
events.analyst_note: CBAM is live in its financial phase from January 2026, but the free allocation phase-out for covered sectors is gradual (2.5% reduction in 2026). Near-term impact on EUA demand is limited. The structural bullish read is for 2028 and beyond, when free allocation reductions accelerate and CBAM certificate demand builds. For now it is more signal than substance.
events.source_label: European Parliament & Council, Regulation (EU) 2023/956 establishing CBAM, 10 May 2023

scenarios.rationale: CBAM's definitive launch proceeds as planned but the near-term EUA demand impact remains muted due to the modest 2.5% free allocation reduction in 2026 and delayed certificate purchases until February 2027.
Market participants may front-run the structural tightening effect by positioning for future years when free allocation reductions accelerate and CBAM compliance costs become fully binding.

Initial implementation challenges or trade disputes could undermine confidence in CBAM's effectiveness whilst the delayed certificate sales timeline reduces immediate compliance pressure on importers.

events.title: EU ETS maritime 100% compliance phase-in
events.summary: From 1 January 2026 shipping companies must surrender EUAs for 100% of verified CO2, up from 70% in 2025. The phase-in is complete. New demand from ~5,000 GT vessels now fully priced into the cap.
events.analyst_note: Effective demand increase of ~30% on the maritime surrender tranche vs 2025. Watch verified emissions data vs allowances surrendered — any shortfall triggers cancellation from the auction calendar.

scenarios.rationale: The mandatory transition to 100% emissions coverage likely drives meaningful structural demand for EUAs as shipping operators must acquire roughly 30% more allowances compared to 2025's 70% requirement.

Strong compliance rates observed in the sector's first year suggest operators may front-load purchases to secure allowances, potentially creating upward price pressure beyond fundamental demand increases.
Market actors may have already incorporated the known phase-in schedule into their purchasing strategies, potentially limiting immediate price impact as the regulatory change was well-telegraphed.

events.title: EU ETS free allocation CBAM phase-out begins
events.summary: From 2026, free allocation to CBAM-covered sectors (iron and steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, hydrogen) begins a gradual phase-out. The CBAM factor is 97.5% in 2026, declining to 51.5% in 2030 and 14% in 2033. This is the first year the phase-out mechanism creates a real demand signal by reducing the volume of allowances freely distributed to covered installations.
events.analyst_note: The phase-out is already law and running. The 2.5% reduction in 2026 is modest in isolation but the trajectory is steep — by 2030 nearly half of current free allocation to these sectors will have been withdrawn. Watch annual verified emissions data from CBAM sectors to gauge how quickly the net demand effect compounds.

scenarios.rationale: The CBAM free allocation phase-out likely proceeds as legislated, with the 2.5% reduction in 2026 creating a modest net demand signal that could support carbon prices modestly.
Market participants may anticipate the accelerating trajectory beyond 2026, with the phase-out reaching 100% by 2034 potentially driving speculative positioning and amplified price responses.
Industry resistance to losing free allowances could lead to regulatory delays or modifications, though the mechanism is already legally established and operational implementation appears on track.

events.title: EU ETS 2 launch (buildings and road transport)
events.summary: A separate EU ETS covering buildings and road transport launches in 2027 with a price corridor of 45-60 EUR/tonne. Distinct from the existing EU ETS; allowances are not fungible. Creates a new compliance market but does not directly affect EUA supply-demand.
events.analyst_note: ETS2 is a separate market, not fungible with EUA. Its launch does not directly affect EUA supply or demand. We flag it as Neutral for EUA precisely because the two systems are ring-fenced. Watch for political pressure to merge or coordinate them post-2030, which would be a material structural change.
events.source_label: European Parliament & Council, Directive (EU) 2023/959, Chapter IVa (ETS2), 10 May 2023

scenarios.rationale: Given ETS2's separate architecture with distinct allowances, the launch likely produces modest spillover effects through regulatory momentum whilst maintaining structural market separation.
Political momentum from successful ETS2 launch could accelerate merger discussions or coordination mechanisms between the systems, creating potential demand linkage expectations.
Market attention may shift towards the newer ETS2 system, potentially reducing trading interest and liquidity in the established EUA market during the transition period.

events.title: CORSIA first mandatory phase (2024-2026)
events.summary: CORSIA transitioned from its voluntary pilot phase to the first mandatory phase covering international aviation emissions growth above the 2019 baseline. 93 states participating. Demand for eligible emissions units (EEUs) will grow through 2026 as airlines accumulate obligations.
events.analyst_note: 93 states are participating. Airline obligations accrue through 2026 and must be settled with eligible emissions units. Demand is real but modest in Phase 1 given the COVID-adjusted baseline. The more important read is on Phase 2 (2027-2035) where obligations grow substantially. Phase 1 is the demand foundation; Phase 2 is the price catalyst.
events.source_label: ICAO, Annex 16 Volume IV (CORSIA SARPs) 2nd Edition, effective 1 January 2024; Assembly Resolution A41-22, October 2022

scenarios.rationale: Growing demand appears likely to materialise modestly as IATA projects 146 to 236 million units for the first phase whilst newly approved standards include Gold Standard, Verra, Climate Action Reserve, and Global Carbon Council expanding supply options.

Stronger price appreciation could emerge if prices jump to $60+ per ton where supply is scarce and continued delays in unlocking host country Letters of Authorisation risk exacerbating regional imbalances.
Modest demand realisation might limit price gains given only one approved project has achieved CEEU issuance whilst compliance obligations remain manageable through the 2028 deadline.

events.title: UK Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism launch
events.summary: The UK government confirmed a Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism launching 1 January 2027, with enabling legislation in the Finance Bill 2025-26. Indirect emissions will not be included until 2029 at earliest.
events.analyst_note: Structural demand anchor for UKA pricing as CBAM aligns import carbon costs with the domestic ETS price. Creates medium-term ceiling on free allocation phase-down speed and supports UKA floor.

scenarios.rationale: Implementation proceeds as planned with CBAM rate reflecting UK ETS pricing and free allowances phased out gradually, providing structural support for UKA demand whilst maintaining market confidence in the mechanism's effectiveness.

Import carbon costs align with domestic ETS prices more aggressively than expected, creating stronger demand fundamentals as market participants anticipate tighter supply-demand dynamics and reduced carbon leakage risks.

Technical implementation challenges or industry pushback could delay certain provisions, though legislation in Finance Bill 2025-26 confirms 1 January 2027 start date remains likely to proceed with potential reduced initial scope.

events.title: UK ETS energy-from-waste voluntary monitoring phase
events.summary: UK ETS is expanding to include energy-from-waste and incineration plants. The voluntary monitoring phase began 1 January 2026 — operators measure, report, and verify emissions to the UK ETS Authority but incur no financial liability yet. Full compliance with surrender obligations begins 1 January 2028.
events.analyst_note: New demand sector entering UK ETS from 2028. The voluntary monitoring phase now running is generating the verified emissions baseline that will determine the size of the demand increase. Watch monitoring data publications from the UK ETS Authority for early indicators of sector-wide emission volumes.

scenarios.rationale: Moderate UKA demand increase appears likely given that the voluntary MRV period is proceeding and will allow the Authority to refine emissions factors and policy design, with estimates suggesting approximately 50% cost increases and £48 per tonne gate fee rises for energy-from-waste operators.

Stronger demand materialisation could occur if the voluntary monitoring reveals higher-than-expected emissions from facilities processing three tonnes or more per hour, particularly as commercial and industrial customers account for around 20% of waste sent to energy-from-waste facilities.

Downside risks include potential waste diversion to cheaper disposal routes such as landfill or refuse-derived fuel export, which could reduce the anticipated demand increase from the energy-from-waste sector.

events.title: California CCA auction reserve price adjustment 2026
events.summary: CARB adjusts the CCA auction reserve price (floor) annually by 5% plus inflation. The 2026 floor is approximately $20.72/tonne. Provides a structural price floor that limits CCA downside and anchors the secondary market.
events.analyst_note: The CCA auction reserve price is adjusted mechanically each year: 5% plus inflation. The 2026 floor is approximately $20.72 per tonne. This is a structural feature, not an event with an uncertain outcome. Confidence is High because the formula is codified and CARB has never deviated from it. It provides a hard price floor that CCA cannot trade through in the primary market, anchoring the secondary market and limiting downside risk for holders.
events.source_label: CARB, 2026 Annual Auction Reserve Price Notice (Cap-and-Trade Regulation, Appendix C), December 2025

scenarios.rationale: The mechanically adjusted reserve price floor of approximately $27.94/tonne likely provides modest upside support as it creates a structural floor for CCA prices, though market sentiment remains mixed regarding regulatory amendments.

The higher price floor may reinforce positive momentum if market participants view the structural support as reducing downside risk whilst CARB's ongoing regulatory amendments create scarcity expectations.

Markets could potentially discount the floor adjustment as already anticipated, with ongoing regulatory uncertainty and potential supply increases from amended rules weighing on sentiment despite the higher floor.

events.title: UK ETS maritime expansion launch
events.summary: The UK government has confirmed 1 July 2026 as the launch date for the UK ETS maritime regime, covering domestic shipping journeys for vessels of 5,000 GT or above. A double-surrender arrangement applies for 2026-2027, with allowances due by 30 April 2028.
events.analyst_note: New demand entrants from mid-2026. Double-surrender in year one means the financial impact is deferred but certain — watch onboarding volumes for Q3 UKA auction demand signal.

scenarios.rationale: The confirmed July launch date with established double-surrender provisions suggests smooth onboarding whilst the 9.3 million allowance cap adjustment provides measured supply response to new demand entrants.

Strong maritime sector compliance with Q3 auction participation could indicate higher-than-expected demand from the 5,000+ GT vessel cohort, particularly given deferred but certain allowance obligations through 2028.
Operational delays in vessel onboarding or lower actual shipping volumes could dampen immediate demand impacts, whilst the double-surrender arrangement delays meaningful price discovery until 2028.

events.title: EU ETS post-2030 public consultation close
events.summary: The European Commission's public consultation on the post-2030 EU ETS implementation package runs from 9 February to 4 May 2026. It explores how ETS-covered emissions interact with national climate targets in the post-2030 framework.
events.analyst_note: Consultation close on 4 May is a soft catalyst — direction of stakeholder submissions informs the July proposal above. Watch for industry positions on scope expansion and removal crediting.

scenarios.rationale: Consultation submissions likely reflect balanced stakeholder views on scope expansion and carbon removal integration, maintaining current regulatory trajectory without significant surprises.

Industry submissions may demonstrate strong support for ambitious carbon removal inclusion and expanded ETS scope, potentially signalling accelerated decarbonisation commitments ahead of July proposals.
Stakeholder feedback could reveal significant concerns over implementation complexity and costs of post-2030 framework changes, potentially moderating Commission ambitions for the July revision.

events.title: UK ETS Auction Reserve Price increase £22→£28
events.summary: The UK ETS Auction Reserve Price rises from £22 to £28, taking effect for the 8 April 2026 auction, an increase of 27%. Mandated by the Greenhouse Gas Emissions Trading Scheme Auctioning (Amendment) Regulations 2026.
events.analyst_note: A hard price floor rise of this magnitude is a direct bullish signal for UKA. From 2027 the ARP increases annually in line with GDP Deflator, locking in real-terms escalation.

scenarios.rationale: The 27% increase to £28 provides a firm price floor that should strengthen UKA values, though market participants may have partially anticipated this mandated adjustment.
The commitment to annual inflation-linked increases creates lasting upward pressure on UKA prices, whilst the substantial 27% rise may trigger additional buying interest from compliance entities.
Current UKA prices around £49 remain well above the new £28 floor, suggesting limited immediate impact if auction demand proves weaker than expected.

events.title: California LCFS first full compliance year under amended regulation
events.summary: 2026 is the first full compliance year under CARB's July 2025 amended LCFS regulation. New carbon intensity benchmarks are in effect: gasoline benchmark dropped from 85.77 to 76.60 gCO2e/MJ and diesel from 86.64 to 81.70. The regulation shifts to CA-GREET 4.0 for new pathway applications. The Auto-Acceleration Mechanism can tighten targets further if the credit bank remains oversupplied, with the earliest trigger date 15 May 2027.
events.analyst_note: Tightening CI benchmarks will erode the existing credit bank over 2026. The pace of erosion determines whether the AAM triggers in 2027 — a further tightening catalyst. Watch quarterly CARB credit balance reports for bank drawdown trajectory. CA-GREET 4.0 transition affects which fuel pathways qualify for credit generation and may reduce supply from some existing projects.

scenarios.rationale: The tightened carbon intensity benchmarks appear likely to continue eroding the credit bank through 2026, with quarterly data already showing the first drawdown since early 2021 and the credit bank standing at 41.54 million MT.

If credit bank conditions deteriorate sufficiently, the Auto-Acceleration Mechanism could trigger on 15 May 2027, advancing carbon intensity benchmarks by one year and further constraining credit supply.
Implementation challenges from the 20% cap on vegetable oil-based fuels and new CA-GREET 4.0 pathway requirements could reduce some credit-generating fuel supplies more slowly than anticipated.

events.title: Article 6.4 carbon market rulebook finalisation
events.summary: Negotiations on the Paris Agreement Article 6.4 mechanism (the new international carbon market) are ongoing. COP31 (2026) is the target for finalising the supervisory body rulebook including methodology standards and corresponding adjustment requirements. Progress is bullish for high-quality VCM credits.
events.analyst_note: Article 6.4 has been in negotiation since Paris. COP31 in Brazil (November 2026) is the target for supervisory body rulebook finalisation. Progress is genuine but slow: methodology standards and corresponding adjustment requirements remain contentious. A finalised rulebook would create a new class of internationally recognised credits that could significantly expand demand for high-quality VCM projects. We see this as the long-run structural driver for premium VCM credit prices.
events.source_label: UNFCCC Article 6.4 Supervisory Body; COP29 Decision on Article 6.4 Mechanism Standards, November 2024

scenarios.rationale: COP31 likely delivers partial rulebook finalisation with key methodology standards completed, though corresponding adjustment requirements may remain under negotiation given ongoing technical complexities.
Brazil's leadership as COP31 host, combined with the Open Coalition momentum and CDM closure pressure, could drive comprehensive rulebook completion with accelerated PACM implementation timeline.
Persistent disagreements over corresponding adjustments and methodology standards could delay comprehensive rulebook finalisation beyond COP31, though some progress would still be expected.

events.title: UK ETS cap review and tightening
events.summary: The UK government is consulting on tightening the UK ETS cap trajectory post-2030 and potentially introducing a carbon price floor. A tighter cap would support UKA prices. Consultation outcome expected H1 2026.
events.analyst_note: The UK government's stated trajectory is tighter caps and a potential carbon price floor, aligned with net zero commitments. A price floor would be structurally bullish, removing UKA downside risk analogous to the CCA auction reserve price. Confidence is Medium because the consultation is ongoing and the timeline has slipped before.
events.source_label: UK ETS Authority (DESNZ), UK ETS Policy Overview; Net Zero Cap effective January 2024

scenarios.rationale: The Authority will seek to consult again on a specific trajectory for the Phase II cap as soon as possible, with the entire second-stage consultation process occurring sufficiently ahead of the beginning of Phase II on 1 January 2031, suggesting a measured approach to cap tightening is likely to proceed as planned.

A more aggressive cap tightening combined with the Authority's decision to retain and inflation-proof the Auction Reserve Price, implementing an inflation-based increase from GBP 22 to GBP 28 in 2026 could provide stronger upward pressure on UKA prices beyond current expectations.

Political resistance to cap tightening or implementation delays could emerge given previous concerns that the proposed range for the cap is not aligned with the availability of technology and infrastructure required for decarbonisation, potentially moderating the bullish impact.

events.title: CORSIA eligible emissions unit standards review
events.summary: ICAO is reviewing which voluntary carbon standards produce credits eligible as CORSIA EEUs. Any expansion of approved standards increases supply (bearish); tightening of quality criteria reduces supply (bullish). Review outcome expected H1 2026.
events.analyst_note: ICAO's review of approved standards is ongoing. Any tightening of quality criteria would reduce eligible supply and lift EEU prices. Any expansion of approved programmes increases supply. We read the direction of travel as toward tighter quality criteria given broader VCM credibility pressures, but the timeline and scope are uncertain. Bullish read is conditional on a tightening outcome.
events.source_label: ICAO Council, CORSIA Eligible Emissions Units Document (updated October 2025); TAB 2024 assessment cycle

scenarios.rationale: ICAO is likely to maintain existing approved standards whilst tightening certain quality criteria incrementally, balancing market supply concerns against credibility pressures.
Stricter quality criteria could meaningfully reduce eligible supply from approved registries, creating supply-demand imbalances that drive EEU prices higher.
ICAO might expand approved methodologies or add new standards to address airlines' compliance needs, potentially increasing eligible supply and dampening price appreciation.

events.title: Verra VCS methodology review for REDD+ credits
events.summary: Verra is revising REDD+ (avoided deforestation) methodologies following credibility challenges in 2023. Revised VM0007 and related standards expected H1 2026. Tighter additionality and leakage requirements are likely to reduce issuance volumes and may require re-verification of existing projects.
events.analyst_note: The 2023 investigative reporting on REDD+ additionality failures triggered a significant credibility crisis for nature-based VCM credits. Verra's revised VM0007 and related standards are expected to materially tighten additionality and leakage requirements. This is bearish for issuance volumes of existing REDD+ projects and likely to require re-verification. It is ultimately bullish for the long-run credibility of the VCM, but the near-term impact is supply contraction and price pressure on lower-quality credits.
events.source_label: Verra, VM0048 Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation (VCS Program), launched 27 November 2023

scenarios.rationale: The VM0048 methodology appears likely to strengthen standards whilst older VM0007 methodologies are excluded from assessment, suggesting moderately tighter requirements may reduce supply by approximately 15-20%.
Data availability by January 2025 and strengthened baseline methodologies may enable smoother transitions than expected, limiting supply disruption to existing project pipelines.

Given REDD projects constitute 24.8% of globally issued credits and face heightened scrutiny affecting five established methodologies, stringent requirements may substantially constrain issuance and trigger widespread re-verification costs.

events.title: UK-EU ETS linking feasibility study
events.summary: The UK and EU committed to a feasibility study on linking the UK ETS and EU ETS
events.analyst_note: The political will exists on both sides but the governance complexity is real: cap-setting, mutual recognition, and regulatory alignment are all contested. A positive feasibility outcome would converge UKA and EUA prices, likely lifting UKA which currently trades at a discount. We rate confidence Medium because a feasibility study outcome is not a linking deal, and actual linking requires further legislation.
events.source_label: UK-EU Summit, Common Understanding on a Renewed Agenda for Cooperation (ETS linkage, Chapter IV), 19 May 2025

scenarios.rationale: The feasibility study may conclude with recommendations for linkage whilst highlighting significant regulatory alignment challenges around cap-setting and governance frameworks that require further negotiation.

A strongly positive feasibility outcome could accelerate political momentum and reduce the substantial €17 per tonne discount that UKA trades at versus EUA, suggesting market confidence in eventual convergence.

The study might reveal irreconcilable differences on regulatory alignment requirements, particularly given concerns about the UK's willingness to align its regulation with that of the bloc, dampening near-term linking prospects.

events.title: CARB Cap-and-Trade post-2030 rulemaking
events.summary: CARB is developing amendments to the Cap-and-Trade regulation for the post-2030 period, including cap trajectory, free allocation methodology, and offset credit provisions. Rulemaking expected to conclude H2 2026. Programme extension to 2045 likely; terms will determine price trajectory.
events.analyst_note: California's Cap-and-Trade programme extension to 2045 is widely expected and has broad legislative support following AB 32 and SB 32. The key variable is the post-2030 cap trajectory and free allocation treatment. More ambitious terms would be structurally bullish for CCA. We rate confidence Medium because CARB rulemaking timelines routinely slip.
events.source_label: CARB, Cap-and-Invest Program Proposed Rulemaking (AB 1207/SB 840), public comment Jan-Mar 2026

scenarios.rationale: CARB likely implements rule changes broadly as proposed with manageable delays, supporting moderate price appreciation through allowance removals whilst maintaining cost containment features.
Rulemaking concludes on schedule with aggressive allowance removals exceeding 118 million tonnes through 2030 and stronger post-2030 caps, driving substantial scarcity premiums.
Rulemaking faces further delays or weakened ambition due to affordability concerns and federal policy uncertainty, dampening near-term price support despite programme extension certainty.

events.title: EU ETS aviation stop-the-clock expiry and CORSIA assessment
events.summary: The stop-the-clock mechanism limiting EU ETS aviation scope to intra-EU flights expires at end of 2026. The European Commission is scheduled to publish a CORSIA assessment by mid-2026 to determine whether it is sufficiently aligned with the Paris Agreement. Subject to the outcome, a legislative proposal could extend EU ETS scope to departing extra-European flights.
events.analyst_note: Outcome is binary and high-impact: a negative CORSIA assessment could trigger EU ETS extension to extra-European departures, materially increasing aviation demand for EUAs. A positive assessment preserves the status quo. Commission report timing (mid-2026) is the near-term catalyst to watch.

scenarios.rationale: A negative CORSIA assessment in mid-2026 leads to a legislative proposal that triggers protracted negotiations, ultimately resulting in partial scope extension to selected extra-European routes rather than comprehensive coverage.

The Commission publishes a definitively negative CORSIA assessment by July 2026, swiftly followed by successful legislative passage of comprehensive EU ETS extension to all departing extra-European flights.
The CORSIA assessment concludes that sufficient improvements have been made to align with Paris Agreement goals, leading to continuation of the stop-the-clock mechanism beyond 2026.

events.title: EPA Renewable Volume Obligation rulemaking 2026-2028
events.summary: The EPA sets annual Renewable Volume Obligations determining how many RINs obligated parties must retire. The 2026-2028 RVO rulemaking is underway; final rule expected Q2 2026. Higher volumes are bullish for D4/D5/D6 RIN prices; Small Refinery Exemption decisions also a key variable.
events.analyst_note: EPA RVO-setting is the primary demand driver for D4, D5, and D6 RINs. Higher RVOs directly increase obligated party demand. The current administration's posture toward biofuels mandates will be the key variable. Small Refinery Exemption decisions can materially reduce effective demand even when headline RVOs are high. We flag this as the single most important near-term regulatory event for RIN prices.
events.source_label: US EPA, Proposed Rule: RFS Standards for 2026 and 2027, 90 Fed. Reg. 25786, 17 June 2025

scenarios.rationale: EPA's proposed RVO increases of 9.5% over 2025-2027 appear broadly achievable given industry capacity, though import RIN reductions may create modest supply tensions.
The proposed 50% import RIN reduction combined with high volume mandates could nearly double RIN prices according to market analysts, particularly benefiting domestic feedstock producers.
Extraordinary compliance costs from aggressive volume targets may undermine biofuel production capacity, potentially leading EPA to moderate final requirements.

events.title: EU Market Stability Reserve review outcome
events.summary: The European Commission is reviewing MSR parameters including the intake rate (currently 24%) and cancellation threshold. Outcome expected Q3 2026. A tightening of parameters would be structurally bullish for EUA; any loosening would weigh on price.
events.analyst_note: The Commission consultation closed July 2025 and a legislative proposal is expected by July 2026. Most stakeholders and the European Parliament favour maintaining the 24% intake rate and the cancellation mechanism. A loosening is politically unlikely given the Clean Industrial Deal narrative. We read the base case as no net change to MSR tightness, with upside risk from additional cancellation provisions.
events.source_label: European Parliament & Council, Decision (EU) 2023/852 amending Decision 2015/1814 (MSR), 19 April 2023

scenarios.rationale: The Commission likely maintains current MSR parameters with stakeholder support and Parliament backing for preserving the 24% intake rate and cancellation mechanism through 2030.

Additional cancellation provisions or enhanced MSR tightness could emerge from the Clean Industrial Deal narrative, particularly given the structural supply deficit and political reluctance to weaken climate ambition.
Any reduction in the intake rate to 12% or loosening of cancellation thresholds remains politically challenging but could occur under extreme industrial competitiveness pressures.

events.title: EU ETS post-2030 Commission revision proposal
events.summary: The 2026 Commission work programme includes a climate package with a proposal to update the EU ETS expected by July 2026. The review covers carbon removals, new sector inclusions, carbon leakage post-CBAM phase-out, and possible linking with other carbon markets.
events.analyst_note: Biggest structural uncertainty for EUA pricing beyond 2030. Scope of carbon removals inclusion and whether aviation free allocation phase-out timeline is hardened are the key variables to watch.

scenarios.rationale: The Commission likely proposes selective reforms addressing carbon removals and aviation whilst avoiding disruptive structural changes that could destabilise the market ahead of the 2030 targets.
Significant tightening through aggressive carbon removals integration and accelerated aviation free allocation phase-out could materially reduce allowance supply and strengthen price signals.
Generous carbon removals crediting or delayed implementation timelines could increase effective allowance supply, whilst sector linking provisions may introduce additional compliance flexibility.

events.title: EU-UK ETS formal linking negotiations
events.summary: Formal EU-UK ETS linking negotiations launched the week of 19 January 2026. EU Climate Commissioner Wopke Hoekstra confirmed both sides are aiming to conclude as soon as possible, with no specific timeline set. The EU Council unanimously backed the negotiating mandate in November 2025; the Commission is now authorised to negotiate. This row tracks the active negotiations phase; milestone rows (technical design, agreement in principle, ratification) will be added as dates are confirmed.
events.analyst_note: The single most structurally important medium-term event for UKA pricing. Convergence with EUA price is the implied outcome of a successful link — UKA currently trades at a material discount. Watch for technical design milestones and any public signals on timeline from either side.

scenarios.rationale: Formal negotiations proceed steadily with technical discussions and working groups established through 2026, though complex regulatory alignment requirements suggest completion by mid-2027.
Political momentum accelerates negotiations with early technical breakthroughs and interim agreements, potentially delivering a functioning link by late 2026 as targeted by both sides.
Technical complexities around regulatory alignment and governance structures prove more challenging than expected, with negotiations extending beyond 2027 and market enthusiasm waning.

events.title: EU ETS annual surrender deadline (2025 emissions)
events.summary: 30 April 2026 is the annual deadline for EU ETS installations and aircraft operators to surrender allowances covering their 2025 verified emissions. A recurring compliance demand event that drives EUA buying pressure through Q1 each year.
events.analyst_note: Near-term bullish price dynamic. Any shortfall vs verified emissions amplifies spot buying. Monitor EU registry data on verified emissions vs outstanding positions into April.

scenarios.rationale: The surrender deadline will likely generate typical seasonal demand pressure as companies acquire allowances to meet their obligations, particularly given the expansion of covered emissions from maritime transport.

Should verified emissions exceed market expectations or if supply constraints coincide with the compliance period, companies may face urgent purchasing requirements for remaining allowances on the open market.
Weaker than anticipated emissions data or ample pre-positioning by operators might reduce the typical surrender period buying pressure, though the structural bullish mechanics remain intact.

events.title: COP31 — Belém, Brazil
events.summary: COP31 takes place in Belém, Brazil in November 2026. Article 6 rules remain not fully settled following COP29. COP31 is the next major deadline for operationalising the Paris Agreement's international carbon market mechanisms (Article 6.2 bilateral agreements and Article 6.4 centralised mechanism). Outcome has direct read-across to CORSIA-eligible credit demand and voluntary market integrity frameworks.
events.analyst_note: Article 6.4 supervisory body progress is the key variable — specifically whether the centralised mechanism begins issuing A6.4ERs at scale in time to influence the CORSIA-eligible supply pool. Bilateral Article 6.2 pipeline is already moving but quality and additionality of ITMOs remains contested. COP31 outcome will either accelerate or further delay the operationalisation timeline that VCM and CORSIA pricing depends on.

scenarios.rationale: COP31 likely delivers incremental progress on Article 6 operationalisation whilst continued bilateral activity generates moderate market expansion without resolving fundamental integrity concerns.

Article 6.4 supervisory body accelerates methodology approvals and registry launch, potentially enabling first A6.4ER issuances by end-2026 whilst enhanced bilateral frameworks boost CORSIA-eligible supply.

Technical implementation delays push A6.4ER issuance beyond 2026 whilst permanence and reversal risk guidance deferrals constrain near-term nature-based solutions financing, dampening market confidence.

events.title: EU ETS Commission statutory review (July 2026)
events.summary: Distinct from the post-2030 proposal (REG-019). The Commission has a statutory obligation to report by end of July 2026 on: negative emissions accounting under EU ETS, feasibility of lowering the 20MW installation threshold, municipal waste incineration inclusion, CCU double-counting, and the functioning of aviation ETS and CORSIA.
events.analyst_note: Hard statutory deadline creates a defined catalyst. Waste incineration inclusion and negative emissions accounting are the variables with the most direct price read-across. CCU double-counting clarification could affect industrial sector compliance costs. Low probability of major structural surprise but watch for scope signals on waste and negative emissions.

scenarios.rationale: The Commission likely delivers a cautiously balanced review that acknowledges technical complexities around negative emissions accounting and waste incineration whilst avoiding major structural commitments ahead of the post-2030 framework.

The review could signal accelerated inclusion of municipal waste incineration from 2028 alongside more generous accounting frameworks for carbon removals, potentially tightening the market through expanded coverage.

Technical and political complexities around waste incineration and negative emissions accounting might lead to delayed implementation timelines or restrictive accounting approaches that reduce market tightening expectations.

events.title: CORSIA Phase 1 end and Phase 2 transition
events.summary: CORSIA mandatory Phase 1 (2024–2026) concludes at end of 2026, marking the first mandatory phase with real airline offsetting obligations for international emissions above the 2020 baseline. The scheme transitions to Phase 2 terms from 2027. Demand outlook through the end of Phase 1 is driven by a handful of major markets, and a supply crunch is emerging in CORSIA-eligible credits.
events.analyst_note: Transition from Phase 1 to Phase 2 introduces uncertainty around baseline recalculation and eligible unit criteria for the new phase. Supply crunch in CORSIA-eligible credits is already visible — the pool of qualifying project vintages is constrained relative to projected Phase 1 offsetting demand. Watch ICAO Technical Advisory Body outputs on Phase 2 methodology and any changes to eligible fuels criteria.

scenarios.rationale: CORSIA Phase 2 transition likely proceeds with baseline methodology adjustments and continued supply constraints, creating modest upward price pressure from enforcement challenges and tight market balance.
Strong enforcement coordination and rapid host country authorisation progress could unlock additional supply whilst Phase 2 expansion drives scope coverage from 64% to 87% of global emissions.
Delayed Phase 2 implementation or significant baseline recalibration alongside continued severe supply shortages with only 15.8 million eligible credits available could depress demand expectations.

cards.label: Compliance allowance selloff signals structural stress
cards.body: The 19.5% weekly drawdown in UKAs is technically significant, extending a three-month decline of 22.6% that now places the instrument at multi-year lows in real terms, while EUAs have lost 23.7% over the same window with no floor mechanism to arrest further downside. Both markets are caught in a structural vacuum: the UK-EU ETS linking study deferred to 2026 removes a key re-rating catalyst, while the MSR intake rate review outcome in Q3 2026 leaves supply-side management parameters unresolved for over a year. In the absence of a cold winter demand spike or a material upward revision to industrial output forecasts, front-month EUA and UKA contracts face continued headwinds. Cross-instrument, the divergence from CORSIA performance reinforces the view that demand certainty, rather than regulatory ambition alone, is the primary price-support variable in the current environment.
cards.source: EU ETS and UK ETS price feeds, MSR review calendar
cards.headline: Compliance allowance selloff signals structural stress

cards.label: CORSIA outperforms on structural demand clarity
cards.body: At plus 12.3% over three months, CORSIA-eligible credits represent the clearest demand-pull dynamic in the current global carbon landscape, with Phase 1 growth in revenue tonne kilometres translating directly into offsetting obligations above the 2019-2020 baseline. The instrument's relative immunity to the compliance allowance selloff reflects its decoupling from power sector and industrial production cycles, which are the primary demand drivers for EUAs and UKAs. Supply-side risk is present ahead of the mid-2026 eligible fuels criteria update, which could tighten or expand the qualifying universe and create spread volatility between CORSIA-eligible and non-eligible project vintages. Simultaneously, the Article 6 bilateral agreement pipeline poses a medium-term redirection risk for nature-based supply currently flowing to voluntary buyers, potentially tightening the CORSIA-eligible pool. Positioning in this instrument requires close monitoring of the ICAO Technical Advisory Body's methodology outputs alongside the VCM's Verra VM0042 update trajectory.
cards.source: CORSIA credit price feed, ICAO CORSIA Phase 1 compliance data, Verra registry
cards.headline: CORSIA outperforms on structural demand clarity

cards.label: US credit markets paralysed by regulatory gaps
cards.body: The flat 1.7% monthly and three-month performance of California LCFS credits reflects a market where participants are unwilling to establish directional exposure ahead of CARB's 2030 carbon intensity benchmark consultation, which has the potential to either restore or permanently impair the credit generation economics for existing fuel pathways. D6 RINs have shown modest monthly recovery but the absence of the EPA's final 2026 RVO rule is preventing obligated parties from forward covering positions with confidence, sustaining basis risk between D3, D4, and D6 categories. The 45Z credit market is effectively non-functional from a price discovery standpoint: without IRS finalisation on the lifecycle emissions calculation model and eligible fuel definitions, offtake agreements cannot be structured with bankable economics, leaving project finance in suspension. Collectively, these three US instruments illustrate how regulatory calendar slippage compounds across interconnected clean fuels policy frameworks, creating correlated uncertainty that suppresses liquidity even when underlying demand fundamentals are constructive. Desks with exposure across LCFS, RINs, and 45Z should expect continued wide bid-ask spreads and limited spot market depth until at least one of these rule processes reaches conclusion.
cards.source: EPA RVO docket, CARB LCFS consultation, IRS 45Z guidance tracker, CME RIN price feed
cards.headline: US credit markets paralysed by regulatory gaps
